chunk_summary: This section outlines a structured clinical approach to assess and triage stunted children, focusing on anthropometry, nutritional phenotypes, proportionality, syndromic cues, and functional screening to guide management and referrals.
learning_objectives: Understand the process of validating anthropometric measurements in stunted children.
Identify and classify nutritional phenotypes, including stunting, wasting, and concurrent conditions.
Recognize proportionality and syndromic cues to differentiate genetic syndromes or skeletal dysplasia.
Perform targeted endocrine, functional, and chronic disease screening in stunted children.
key_facts: Stunting is defined as height-for-age Z-score (HAZ) < -2.
MUAC (mid-upper arm circumference) is critical for children aged 6–59 months.
Concurrent wasting and stunting (WaSt) increases urgency for stabilization pathways.
Proportionality assessment includes upper-to-lower segment ratio and limb length.
Endocrine screening includes thyroid enlargement, pubertal staging, and blood pressure checks.
Danger signs like nutritional edema or very low MUAC require immediate stabilization.
document_key_topics: Classification of nutritional phenotypes in stunted children
Proportionality and syndromic assessment for genetic or skeletal conditions
Endocrine and functional screening in chronic undernutrition
